Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I'd spend around 25 hours a week studying, but most of it happened outside the LMS through podcasts, recorded lectures I'd download, and discussions with study groups. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to material while commuting or doing other things. The LMS was more of a checkpoint for me rather than my primary study space.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied quite a bit. I wouldn't say I had set times each week—it was more about when I could absorb information best. Sometimes I'd have intense listening sessions over a weekend, other times I'd spread it throughout the week. Deadlines did push me to check in more, but my actual learning happened on my own schedule.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I needed to check announcements, see if new materials were posted, or when an assignment deadline was approaching. Sometimes I'd pop in to see discussion threads or grab lecture recordings to download. It wasn't a daily habit for me—more like periodic check-ins to make sure I wasn't missing anything critical.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine the way other tools did. I'd use it when necessary, but I found myself gravitating toward external resources and my own downloaded materials more often. My routine centered around listening and audio-based study, which the LMS didn't really facilitate well.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could download them and listen to the audio portions repeatedly. The discussion forums were also helpful—I posted quite a bit there because hearing different perspectives helped me process concepts. Anything I could convert into an auditory experience worked well for my learning style.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials on the LMS because I'd find audio versions or podcasts covering similar content elsewhere. Some of the interactive tasks felt tedious to me, so I'd delay them or only skim through. If something couldn't be listened to or discussed verbally, it just didn't stick as well for me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it helped me actually understand and retain the material. Discussion posts were valuable because articulating ideas out loud—even in writing—helped solidify my understanding. If I could tell an activity would show up on the exam or deepen my comprehension, I'd engage with it fully.
Q8: Optional activities felt like bonuses that I could explore if they interested me, while required ones had this weight of obligation. I'd sometimes engage more genuinely with optional content because there was no pressure. Required activities sometimes felt like boxes to check rather than genuine learning opportunities.
Q9: Absolutely—this was my primary mode of studying. I'd download lecture recordings and convert them to audio files for my phone. I used textbooks, external podcasts, and even YouTube videos extensively. My study group would meet up and discuss concepts verbally, which was incredibly valuable for me as an auditory learner.
Q10: Most of my studying wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. When I'd spend hours listening to downloaded lectures on repeat during my commute, that's invisible. My study group discussions, re-listening to recordings while exercising, reviewing my voice memos of key concepts—none of that registers anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video once, download it, and then listen to the audio version maybe five or six more times over the following days. The LMS would show one view, but I'd actually engaged with that material extensively. This was my standard approach for almost all the course content.
Q12: There were stretches where my attendance dropped significantly because I was dealing with other commitments. Sometimes the material felt dry when presented in text-heavy formats, which made it harder to engage. I'd feel surprised when I realized how much I'd fallen behind on the LMS tasks even though I felt I understood the material.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When life got busy, the LMS was the first thing I'd deprioritize because I could still learn through my audio-based methods. Fatigue made it hard to sit and read through online materials, so I'd default to listening instead, which doesn't show up in the system.
Q14: Sometimes I'd scroll through the LMS checking boxes mentally without really absorbing anything. I'd open resources just to mark them as viewed or to see what was there. Those moments felt hollow—I was present in the system but not actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments more than I should have—my completion rate shows that. I'd prioritize understanding the concepts over completing the paperwork, which hurt my assignment scores. It's something I struggle with because the learning felt more important than proving I'd learned through written work.
Q16: Deadlines would trigger bursts of LMS activity from me. I'd suddenly log in multiple times, check requirements, and try to catch up. Without deadlines, I'd drift away from the platform for longer stretches. They were necessary external pressure that kept me somewhat accountable to the system.
Q17: Feedback did motivate me when it was constructive and specific. After receiving good exam scores, I felt validated that my unconventional study methods were working. But feedback on assignments sometimes felt disconnected from my actual understanding, which was frustrating.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation in that I'd engage more when there were clear audio or discussion components. When the structure was heavily text-based or required constant LMS presence, I'd struggle to maintain engagement. I adapted the structure to fit my learning style rather than the other way around.
Q19: Having downloadable video content made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or extensive reading within the platform. More audio-friendly options would have helped someone like me engage more consistently through the LMS.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for what they need, exploring content, maybe feeling overwhelmed by options. They could be like me—gathering materials to study offline but struggling to translate that engagement into submitted work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score was excellent despite low LMS engagement because I was learning intensively through other means. The LMS captured maybe 20% of my actual study effort. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing at all.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how often they clicked around. But they completely miss offline engagement, the quality of understanding, and alternative study methods. They capture presence in a system, not presence of mind or depth of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively processing and internalizing the material, regardless of where or how that happens. It's about genuine curiosity, making connections, and being able to apply what I've learned. For me, engagement looks like listening to lectures repeatedly and discussing ideas with peers—not necessarily clicking through an LMS.
Q24: My entire auditory learning process is invisible to the LMS. The hours I spent listening to downloaded content, my study group discussions, the voice memos I made summarizing concepts—none of that shows up. The LMS data would suggest I was barely engaged when I was actually deeply immersed in the material through my own methods.
